input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin is built following Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) principles. Functional and logical structures are drafted in SysML, with component–connector views expressed in ADLs such as MontiArc (or AADL, ACME). Behavioural dynamics are modeled via state‑machine/automaton viewpoints in MontiArc or SysML v1.7 and subsequently simulated with MathWorks Simulink. The constellation also incorporates analytical models implemented in C++ or Java for code‑level simulation. Data originating from the physical asset is streamed into the twin through a one‑way “Digital Shadow” – an automated collection of contextual data traces. The DT ingests these traces, aligns them with the model hierarchy, and stores them in a structured repository, allowing correlation between the geometric, behavioural and analytical representations for validation and optimization.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The DT lifecycle follows the MBSE multi‑level abstraction model described in Section II.B.1. Initial requirements are captured in the requirement layer using SysML requirement diagrams. Functional behaviour is expressed in the functional architecture layer through SysML activity and sequence diagrams, potentially enriched by MontiArc automaton or SysML state machines. The logical architecture is then articulated with ADLs (ACME, AADL, MontiArc) which provide component‑connector views and detailed interface definitions. Finally, at the product/technical layer the DT is instantiated as executable code – typically generated from SysML or MontiArc models and implemented in C++ or Java – and integrated with simulation environments such as MathWorks Simulink for dynamic validation. Transition between stages is managed by traceability links inherent in the MBSE toolchain (e.g., SysML requirement to function, to component, to code). Governance of the lifecycle relies on MBSE best practices, including version control of models, change management, and model‑to‑code consistency checks. This staged, traceable approach ensures that each abstraction level receives a dedicated representation, and that evolution from conception to deployment is orchestrated through rigorous documentation and tool support.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The development of a DT within the framework presented in the document is grounded in Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE). Methods such as RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT are explicitly referenced (see Section II.B.1). These methodologies drive the iterative engineering of the DT through the stages described above. Quality assurance relies on the inherent validation capabilities of the modeling languages: SysML activity/state machines and MontiArc automaton provide formal behavior specifications, while Simulink models enable simulation‑based testing of dynamic aspects. Code generation from SysML/UML models to C++ or Java ensures that the implementation remains consistent with the specifications. Evolution management is handled by maintaining versioned model artefacts in a model repository; traceability links between requirements, functions, and code allow rollback and impact analysis. The process emphasizes continuous verification and validation at each abstraction level, leveraging simulation tools such as MathWorks Simulink and code‑generation workflows to detect regressions early.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The DT specification in the document adheres to several widely accepted industry modeling standards. System representation is performed with SysML, a standardized systems modeling language that supports requirement, activity, sequence, and state machine diagrams. Component‑connector views are expressed using architecture description languages such as ACME, AADL, and MontiArc, each of which provides formal semantic descriptions of interfaces and interconnections. The integration of state‐machine based behavior is supported by SysML activity/state machine diagrams and MontiArc Automaton, enabling formal verification. Additionally, the document references SMArDT, an MBSE methodology that incorporates best‑practice artefacts aligned with functional safety and industrial safety standards, thus facilitating interoperability with existing engineering environments and compliance with established model‑based development guidelines.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.
